citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
During the inspection, two zebu were observed to have hooves that were excessively long and need to be trimmed.
Because the hooves were excessively long, the weight of the animals’ bodies seemed to be shifted to the back of the
hoof, as opposed to being evenly weight bearing, and the front hooves appeared to protrude forward instead of pointed
towards the ground. One of the zebus appeared to walk mildly gingerly when placing the left front hoof. The facility stated
that the hoofstock had their hooves trimmed in the fall and had not identified hooves that needed to be trimmed again.
Overgrown hooves can cause pain and discomfort, and lead to abnormal gaits. Inadequate daily observations can lead to
the prolonged suffering of animals when changes in their health are not noticed in a timely manner. The licensee must
ensure that thorough daily observations are conducted and, even with scheduled hoof trims, daily observations are
conducted to ensure that hoof care is performed in a timely manner to prevent overgrowth.
Correct by March 30, 2026.

Handling of animals at the facility was not done as expeditiously and carefully as possible, leading to the death of a
turaco. On Jan. 12, 2026, an employee was exiting the interior door of the enclosure housing two turacos and two toucans
when one turaco flew at the door and was accidentally shut in the door. The bird sustained injury and was humanely
euthanized that day.
Failure to handle animals appropriately can lead to trauma, behavioral stress, and physical harm. Correct by ensuring that
all animals are handled as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma, overheating,
excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort.
Correct by March 28, 2026. The facility has instituted corrective actions before the inspection.

The following enclosures have not been maintained in good repair due to the presence of sharp points:
• The primary enclosure housing two Bactrian and one dromedary camels has a wooden grain feeder attached to
the wall. One of the wooden bars has been worn away, exposing two screws near the area the animals would bend down
to eat.
• An exposed nail approximately two inches in length is sticking out of the wall of the camel pen in the barn.
• The wooden threshold leading out of the goat pen to the goat yard has worn away, exposing a sharp screw.
Goats will step on this threshold to exit and enter their indoor pen.
Primary enclosures must be maintained in good repair to prevent injury to the animals contained in the enclosure. The
indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the
animals from injury and to contain the animals.

Two Capybaras are currently housed in the otter indoor building for the winter. The enclosures do not provide a pool or
water feature for the capybaras, other than the animals’ drinking water. Capybaras are a semi aquatic species, and
require normal postural adjustments include defecating in water and swimming. The current enclosure design does not
allow for normal postural movement for this semi aquatic species which would include submersion in water to defecate
and swimming behaviors. These animals should be provided access to a water source that allows and promotes natural
postural and social adjustments and to promote healthy defecation which can reduce the risk of gastrointestinal issues.
Enclosures shall be constructed and maintained so as to provide sufficient space to allow each animal to make normal
postural and social adjustments with adequate freedom of movement.
Correct by March 30, 2026

The facility is not cleaning as often as necessary to prevent contamination of the animals. The zebu enclosure containing
two zebu has a three-sided shelter structure with several inches of wet manure and mud that does not offer any dry or
clean space for the zebu to lie down. There is no other shelter in the enclosure. The back legs of one zebu was covered in
muck hoof to approximately mid-femur. The licensee stated that the shelter had not been mucked out since the start of
winter.
The excreta in the zebu shelter shall be removed to prevent such accumulations. Excreta shall be removed as often as
necessary to prevent contamination of the animals contained therein and to minimize disease hazards and to reduce
odors.
Correct by March 30, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
During the inspection, two zebu were observed to have hooves that were excessively long and need to be trimmed. 
Because the hooves were excessively long, the weight of the animals’ bodies seemed to be shifted to the back of the 
hoof, as opposed to being evenly weight bearing, and the front hooves appeared to protrude forward instead of pointed 
towards the ground. One of the zebus appeared to walk mildly gingerly when placing the left front hoof. The facility stated 
that the hoofstock had their hooves trimmed in the fall and had not identified hooves that needed to be trimmed again.  
 
Overgrown hooves can cause pain and discomfort, and lead to abnormal gaits. Inadequate daily observations can lead to 
the prolonged suffering of animals when changes in their health are not noticed in a timely manner. The licensee must 
ensure that thorough daily observations are conducted and, even with scheduled hoof trims, daily observations are 
conducted to ensure that hoof care is performed in a timely manner to prevent overgrowth.  
 
Correct by March 30, 2026. 
2.131(b)(1)  Critical
   
Handling of animals. 
Handling of animals at the facility was not done as expeditiously and carefully as possible, leading to the death of a 
turaco. On Jan. 12, 2026, an employee was exiting the interior door of the enclosure housing two turacos and two toucans 
when one turaco flew at the door and was accidentally shut in the door. The bird sustained injury and was humanely 
euthanized that day.  
 
Failure to handle animals appropriately can lead to trauma, behavioral stress, and physical harm. Correct by ensuring that 
all animals are handled as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma, overheating, 
excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort. 
 
Correct by March 28, 2026. The facility has instituted corrective actions before the inspection. 
3.125(a)  Repeat

Facilities, general. 
The following enclosures have not been maintained in good repair due to the presence of sharp points:  
•  The primary enclosure housing two Bactrian and one dromedary camels has a wooden grain feeder attached to 
the wall. One of the wooden bars has been worn away, exposing two screws near the area the animals would bend down 
to eat.  
•  An exposed nail approximately two inches in length is sticking out of the wall of the camel pen in the barn.  
•  The wooden threshold leading out of the goat pen to the goat yard has worn away, exposing a sharp screw. 
Goats will step on this threshold to exit and enter their indoor pen.  
 
Primary enclosures must be maintained in good repair to prevent injury to the animals contained in the enclosure. The 
indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the 
animals from injury and to contain the animals. 
3.128 
   
Space requirements. 
Two Capybaras are currently housed in the otter indoor building for the winter. The enclosures do not provide a pool or 
water feature for the capybaras, other than the animals’ drinking water. Capybaras are a semi aquatic species, and 
require normal postural adjustments include defecating in water and swimming. The current enclosure design does not 
allow for normal postural movement for this semi aquatic species which would include submersion in water to defecate 
and swimming behaviors. These animals should be provided access to a water source that allows and promotes natural 
postural and social adjustments and to promote healthy defecation which can reduce the risk of gastrointestinal issues.  
 
Enclosures shall be constructed and maintained so as to provide sufficient space to allow each animal to make normal 
postural and social adjustments with adequate freedom of movement. 
 
Correct by March 30, 2026 
3.131(a) 
   
Sanitation. 
The facility is not cleaning as often as necessary to prevent contamination of the animals. The zebu enclosure containing 
two zebu has a three-sided shelter structure with several inches of wet manure and mud that does not offer any dry or 
clean space for the zebu to lie down. There is no other shelter in the enclosure. The back legs of one zebu was covered in 
muck hoof to approximately mid-femur. The licensee stated that the shelter had not been mucked out since the start of 
winter.  
 
 
The excreta in the zebu shelter shall be removed to prevent such accumulations. Excreta shall be removed as often as 
necessary to prevent contamination of the animals contained therein and to minimize disease hazards and to reduce 
odors. 
 
Correct by March 30, 2026.

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
